questions.text: I feel that my confidence in decisions helps reassure the people around me.
I feel that good leadership means making the call, not facilitating the call.
I notice that emotionally significant dates — anniversaries, milestones — produce strong bodily reactions even years later.
I believe that short-term pleasure has inherent value that shouldn't always be sacrificed for the future.
I notice when someone leans slightly away from a person they distrust.
I believe that my physical fitness routine should take priority over social commitments.
I hold the view that the people who do the most good in crises are those who can maintain analytical distance from the suffering around them.
I think that emotional restraint during crises is a skill that produces measurably better outcomes for the people involved.
I notice the difference between someone who is quiet because they are content and someone who is quiet because they are upset.
I tend to miss the social implications of who gets credit for an idea in a group.
I believe that someone who takes command earns the right to shape the outcome.
I focus on what's interesting and energizing right now rather than optimizing for the long term.
I tend to protect my personal time fiercely.
I feel a strong pull to ensure the agenda reflects everyone's interests, not just mine.
I hold the view that my moral commitment to addressing suffering is best expressed through reliable, sustained action rather than through emotional intensity.
I experience transitions — new cities, new routines, new people — with curiosity rather than upheaval.
I tend to give the largest portion to others and take whatever remains.
I find that my body is a poor indicator of my emotional state because it rarely changes.
I prefer to find a path everyone can accept rather than force a choice.
I find that assembling care packages for people going through hard times comes naturally to me.
I prefer that the narrative around a project's progress reflects everyone's perspective.
I feel that a predictable, well-ordered life is a peaceful life.
I find that I generally do not notice when someone is managing my impression of a third party.
I do not naturally sense when a person is gauging whether I am trustworthy before opening up.
I work on the current version and don't worry about preserving past iterations.
I find that loud noises, surprises, or sudden changes don't produce a big reaction in me.
I think about what tools or supplies I'll need before starting a project.
I think of my daily habits as investments that pay dividends in the years ahead.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is a moral reality that persists even when circumstances make responding difficult, costly, or inconvenient.
I find that engaging with human pain through an analytical framework rather than an emotional one allows me to act with greater precision and less bias.
I find that my nervous system treats change like an emergency, even when the change is positive.
I hold the view that feeling things intensely is simply how I'm wired.
I believe that the most important thing I can do with my life is reduce others' burdens.
I sense when a shared meal or coffee is actually a pretext for a conversation someone needs to have.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the influence that has done the most to shape the kind of friend, colleague, and community member I strive to be.
I believe that the energy I spend on myself comes back multiplied.
